planting.note: Sow 4–6 weeks before last frost date; Telephone is a tall climber reaching 5–6 feet and requires sturdy support — grow on strong netting or trellis strung between tall posts.

directions: Sow seed 1–2 inches deep and 2–3 inches apart in cool, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun, 4–6 weeks before last frost; Telephone pea (sold in Britain as Alderman) is the classic tall heirloom main-crop shell pea, a vigorous climber to 5–6 feet that produces large, curved dark-green pods with 8–10 sweet, tender peas per pod — significantly more productive per plant than bush varieties and the pod size that made it famous in Victorian kitchen gardens. Requires strong netting or pea sticks for support. Harvest when pods are well-filled and peas are young and sweet; productivity drops rapidly if pods are left to mature fully on the vine.

planting.note: Sow in early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost; a tall climber to 4–5 feet requiring netting or trellis support; harvest young pods before seeds begin to swell for best eating quality.

directions: Sow seed 1–2 inches deep in cool, fertile soil in full sun 4–6 weeks before last frost, providing 4–5 feet of netting or trellis support; Mammoth Melting Sugar is the most widely grown heirloom snow pea, an heirloom variety documented since at least the early 1900s producing large (4–6 inch), flat, tender pods with a delicate, melt-in-the-mouth texture when harvested young and cooked lightly in stir-fries or eaten raw. The pods are stringless when young and the standard against which other snow peas are judged for tenderness and flavor. Harvest when pods are 3–4 inches long and absolutely flat with no seed bulge visible for the premium eating experience.

common_name: Dwarf Grey Sugar Pea (Snow Pea, Grey Sugar)

planting.note: One of the earliest snow peas to mature; sow very early in spring (8 weeks before last frost) for a quick cool-season harvest before summer heat arrives; compact habit needs only minimal support (24–30 in twiggy branches or short netting).

directions: Sow seed 1–2 inches deep in early spring (up to 8 weeks before last frost) in cool, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Dwarf Grey Sugar is an old heirloom snow pea, documented since at least the 1800s, distinguished by its unusually attractive purple-tinged vines and flowers against blue-grey foliage — the purplish cast coming from anthocyanins that also make the young tendrils and pods slightly reddish before the pods mature to green. Compact habit (24–30 inches) makes it ideal for small gardens and containers. An early and productive snow pea; harvest pods frequently when flat and young before seeds swell.

planting.note: Sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; a tall climber (4–5 ft) with attractive purple flowers requiring trellis support; pods change from bright yellow-green to full yellow at harvest — pick when flat and yellow-green.

directions: Sow seed 1–2 inches deep in cool, fertile soil in full sun 4–6 weeks before last frost with trellis support; Golden Sweet is a distinctive heirloom snow pea from India with vivid lemon-yellow pods against deep green foliage and attractive purple flowers — one of the most ornamental edible peas available. The flavor of the bright yellow flat pods is sweet and delicate with a slightly more tender texture than green snow peas; they make a visually striking addition to salads and stir-fries. Harvest pods when fully yellow and flat before seeds begin to swell for best quality. The yellow color provides an interesting contrast to green and purple-podded pea varieties grown together.
